biases_detected: shell_tool_preference_violations:RECURRING:IMPROVING — 2/11 units (18%; 4a3e88a3-1,4a3e88a3-24); 4a3e88a3-1: ls-via-Bash x2, find-via-Bash x2, grep-via-Bash x2 at session start for file discovery; 4a3e88a3-24: grep-E-via-Bash as pipe filter (node ... 2>&1 | grep -E); rate 18% is below historical lower bound of 48%; session context (data pipeline heavy in node inline scripts) partially explains reduction; session-start file-discovery sub-pattern in unit 1 persists structurally unchanged across all measured cycles

cp_overwrite_failure_hook_interaction:NEW — unit 4a3e88a3-24: cp chain success=false then cp -f success=false then command cp success=true; first attempt lacked -f; second added -f but also failed; third used command prefix without -f and succeeded; if safe-delete hook intercepts cp -f on existing-destination files the -f flag is counterproductive; pattern is structurally distinct from path_assumption_bias (paths were correct) and from shell_tool_preference_violations (cp has no dedicated tool equivalent); not recorded in any prior calibration cycle
recommendations: shell_tool_preference_violations: no new recommendation — already flagged and elevated across 40+ cycles with zero internalization; apparent rate improvement this cycle (18% vs 48-92% historical) should be monitored across sessions with similar data-pipeline character before concluding improvement; session-start file-discovery pattern (ls/find/grep via Bash in unit 1) remains the most consistent sub-pattern and the easiest hook target

cp_overwrite_failure_hook_interaction: NEW finding — investigate whether safe-delete hook intercepts cp -f invocations on existing destination files; if confirmed, document that command cp (bypassing shell function aliases) is the correct escape and that -f flag alone does not resolve it; this is actionable for a hook or CLAUDE.md annotation
